title: Use semantic search for relationship discovery
state_description: When question asks for a relationship between entities (e.g., spouse, stadium, coach)

action.description: First try semantic search with natural language keywords related to the relationship.
steps: Use SearchGraphPatterns with entity ID and semantic keyword
If multiple results, check for temporal or specificity filters
If semantic search fails or returns irrelevant results, switch to direct SPARQL

avoid: Don't rely solely on semantic search for ambiguous or temporal queries; verify with direct predicates.
embedding_text: Use semantic search for relationship discovery When question asks for a relationship between entities (e.g., spouse, stadium, coach) First try semantic search with natural language keywords related to the relationship. relationship spouse stadium coach president judge

title: Switch to direct SPARQL when semantic search fails
state_description: When semantic search returns irrelevant results, empty results, or wrong entities

action.description: If semantic search fails to return correct answer, try ExecuteSPARQL with known direct predicates for the domain.
steps: Identify likely predicate based on question type
Use ExecuteSPARQL with direct predicate (e.g., sports.sports_team.arena_stadium)
If still wrong, try alternative predicates or add filters

avoid: Don't repeat the same semantic search expecting different results; change strategy.
embedding_text: Switch to direct SPARQL when semantic search fails When semantic search returns irrelevant results, empty results, or wrong entities If semantic search fails to return correct answer, try ExecuteSPARQL with known direct predicates for the domain. empty result wrong result irrelevant semantic search

title: Apply temporal filters for 'current' or year-specific questions

action.description: Add temporal filters to SPARQL queries using date properties and ORDER BY/LIMIT or FILTER.
steps: Include date variable in SPARQL pattern
Use ORDER BY DESC(?date) LIMIT 1 for 'current'
Use FILTER(?date = year) for specific year
If temporal filter returns empty, check predicate names for historical vs current

avoid: Don't assume semantic search will handle temporal constraints; explicitly add them in SPARQL.
embedding_text: Apply temporal filters for 'current' or year-specific questions Question includes temporal qualifiers like 'current', '2010', 'in 1996' Add temporal filters to SPARQL queries using date properties and ORDER BY/LIMIT or FILTER. current year 1996 2010 now

title: Fix SPARQL syntax errors with proper prefixes and triple structure
state_description: SPARQL execution fails with syntax error or missing prefix error

action.description: When SPARQL syntax error occurs, ensure proper ns: prefix for all predicates and break complex patterns into basic triple patterns.
steps: Add ns: prefix to all predicate names
Replace arrow syntax with explicit triple patterns (e.g., ?s ns:pred ?o . ?o ns:pred2 ?result)
Test query step by step

avoid: Don't use arrow syntax or shorthand; always use full triple patterns with ns: prefixes.
embedding_text: Fix SPARQL syntax errors with proper prefixes and triple structure SPARQL execution fails with syntax error or missing prefix error When SPARQL syntax error occurs, ensure proper ns: prefix for all predicates and break complex patterns into basic triple patterns. syntax error missing prefix ns: arrow syntax

title: Handle multi-hop relationships with intermediate variables
state_description: Answer requires traversing multiple predicates (e.g., court -> judges -> tenure -> judge name)

action.description: For multi-hop relationships, break into separate triple patterns with intermediate variables.
steps: Identify each hop in the relationship chain
Create separate triple patterns for each hop
Connect patterns via shared variables
Select final variable as answer

avoid: Don't try to combine multiple hops into one predicate; they are separate relations in the graph.
embedding_text: Handle multi-hop relationships with intermediate variables Answer requires traversing multiple predicates (e.g., court -> judges -> tenure -> judge name) For multi-hop relationships, break into separate triple patterns with intermediate variables. multi-hop intermediate chain judges

title: Validate entity linking with type checking
state_description: Semantic search returns entities of wrong type (e.g., city instead of country, coach instead of team)

action.description: When results seem unrelated to question, verify entity types using SearchTypes or add type constraints to SPARQL.
steps: Use SearchTypes to check entity type
Add type triple to SPARQL (e.g., ?entity ns:type.object.type ns:sports.sports_team)
If type mismatch, search with different semantic keywords

avoid: Don't assume entity ID directly corresponds to answer type; verify with type predicates.
embedding_text: Validate entity linking with type checking Semantic search returns entities of wrong type (e.g., city instead of country, coach instead of team) When results seem unrelated to question, verify entity types using SearchTypes or add type constraints to SPARQL. wrong type city country coach team

title: Use containedby for geographical location questions
state_description: Question asks for country/region of a place (birth country, stadium location)

action.description: For geographical containment, use containedby or location predicates to find parent region.
steps: Find place entity via semantic search
Use containedby or location.location.containedby predicate
May need multiple hops (city -> region -> country)

avoid: Don't assume place name directly maps to country; use containment relationships.
embedding_text: Use containedby for geographical location questions Question asks for country/region of a place (birth country, stadium location) For geographical containment, use containedby or location predicates to find parent region. country location birthplace containedby

title: Avoid self-referential results in relationship queries
state_description: Relationship query returns the entity itself or unrelated entities

action.description: Add filters to exclude self-reference and ensure proper direction of relationship.
steps: Add FILTER(?answer != ?entity) to exclude self
Check predicate direction (subject vs object)
Verify predicate semantics match question intent

avoid: Don't assume all relationship predicates are symmetric; direction matters.
embedding_text: Avoid self-referential results in relationship queries Relationship query returns the entity itself or unrelated entities Add filters to exclude self-reference and ensure proper direction of relationship. self unrelated includes self

title: Try alternative predicates when first attempt fails
state_description: First predicate attempt returns empty results or wrong answers

action.description: If first predicate fails, try alternative predicates from related domains (e.g., sports.sports_team_coach_tenure vs american_football.football_historical_coach_position).
steps: List possible predicates for the domain
Try each alternative systematically
Use SearchGraphPatterns without semantic to discover predicates

avoid: Don't give up after one failed predicate; Freebase has multiple ways to represent similar relationships.
embedding_text: Try alternative predicates when first attempt fails First predicate attempt returns empty results or wrong answers If first predicate fails, try alternative predicates from related domains (e.g., sports.sports_team_coach_tenure vs american_football.football_historical_coach_position). empty result alternative predicate different domain

title: Use SearchGraphPatterns without semantic to discover predicates
state_description: When unsure which predicate to use or semantic search fails

action.description: Use SearchGraphPatterns with just entity ID (no semantic) to see all available predicates and values.
steps: Call SearchGraphPatterns with sparql=entity_id only
Examine results for relevant predicates
Select appropriate predicate for SPARQL query

avoid: Don't guess predicate names; let the system show available options.
embedding_text: Use SearchGraphPatterns without semantic to discover predicates When unsure which predicate to use or semantic search fails Use SearchGraphPatterns with just entity ID (no semantic) to see all available predicates and values. discover predicate unknown predicate explore

title: Handle literal values in SPARQL with proper formatting
state_description: SPARQL fails when using literal string values without proper prefix or datatype

action.description: For literal values in SPARQL, either use ns: prefix for URIs or proper quotes for string literals.
steps: If value is a URI, add ns: prefix
If value is a string literal, use quotes
If unsure, try both approaches

avoid: Don't mix URI and literal syntax; be consistent with value type.
embedding_text: Handle literal values in SPARQL with proper formatting SPARQL fails when using literal string values without proper prefix or datatype For literal values in SPARQL, either use ns: prefix for URIs or proper quotes for string literals. literal string prefix error ns:

title: Prioritize recent/current results for ambiguous entities
state_description: Multiple results returned where question implies most recent or current

action.description: When multiple answers exist, use temporal ordering or current status filters to select most relevant.
steps: Add ORDER BY DESC(?date) LIMIT 1 for temporal queries
For 'current' questions, filter by end date being unbound or future
Use domain knowledge to select appropriate answer

avoid: Don't return first result arbitrarily; apply logical selection criteria.
embedding_text: Prioritize recent/current results for ambiguous entities Multiple results returned where question implies most recent or current When multiple answers exist, use temporal ordering or current status filters to select most relevant. multiple ambiguous current recent
